framework.reasoning: The paper explicitly describes gradients backpropagated through the Euler-Maruyama SDE solver via the reparametrisation trick, mixed-precision training, and 'differentiable convex optimization layers (Agrawal et al. 2019)' -- a direct reference to cvxpylayers, which is PyTorch-native (also has a JAX variant but the paper's overall description -- standard nn.Module-style drift/diffusion MLPs, Hugging Face for Chronos-2 -- points to PyTorch as the far more natural fit). Hugging Face transformers is explicitly named for loading the Chronos-2 baseline. CUDA is required for practical training given 6 models x 4 datasets x 5 seeds x (up to) 7 ablation variants, and Jane Street alone has ~12M rows.

module_hierarchy.description: Dataset-specific preprocessing pipelines for Jane Street, Optiver, Time-IMM, and DSLOB (Section 3). Produces windowed tensors + masks shared by all neural models.

public_methods.name: load_and_fill

public_methods.name: build_windows

public_methods.returns: {'theta':.., 'mu':.., 'sigma':..} MLE-fitted, then amplified (theta*1.5, mu*1.2)

public_methods.signature: fit_garch(self, log_returns: np.ndarray) -> dict
public_methods.returns: {'omega':.., 'alpha':.., 'beta':..} GARCH(1,1), then amplified (beta'=min(0.95,1.1*beta), alpha'=1.2*alpha)

public_methods.signature: generate_synthetic_features(self, seed_features: np.ndarray, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: VAR(1)-correlated-noise-augmented synthetic 85-feature panel, time-warped via a GP-sampled deformation

public_methods.signature: validate_synthetic(self, seed, synthetic) -> dict
public_methods.returns: {'ks_pvalue':.., 'acf_diff':.., 'corr_matrix_diff':.., 'tail_pct_diff':..} per paper's stated validation checks

module_hierarchy.description: Module 1: continuous-time encoder (Section 4.1, Appendix A.2). ASSUMED d_z=64 per Figure 3 caption hint (SIR confidence 0.35).

public_methods.name: __init__
public_methods.signature: __init__(self, d_x: int, d_z: int = 64, n_poles: int = 16, n_fourier: int = 8)
public_methods.returns: initializes learnable poles lambda_k (Re<0 via softplus reparam), residue matrices A_k, and Fourier-embedding bias MLP

public_methods.name: kernel
public_methods.signature: kernel(self, delta_t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: kappa(delta_t) = sum_k A_k * exp(lambda_k * delta_t), [*, d_z, d_x]

public_methods.name: forward
public_methods.signature: forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor, eval_times: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: z [*, M, d_z] latent function evaluated at eval_times via left-Riemann-sum discretization of the kernel integral (Eq. 4)

module_hierarchy.description: Module 2: economics-informed latent dynamics (Section 4.2, Appendix A.3).

public_methods.name: forward
public_methods.signature: forward(self, z: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: mu_theta(z,t) [*, d_z], 1-hidden-layer MLP with tanh activation on [z; TimeEmbedding(t)]

public_methods.name: forward
public_methods.signature: forward(self, z: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: sigma_phi(z,t) [*, d_z, d_w] = L_phi @ D_phi, Cholesky-factorized SPD-guaranteeing construction (Appendix A.3.2)

public_methods.name: __init__
public_methods.signature: __init__(self, d_z: int, d_w: int, drift: DriftNet, diffusion: DiffusionNet)
public_methods.returns: wraps drift+diffusion for Euler-Maruyama simulation

public_methods.name: simulate
public_methods.signature: simulate(self, z0: torch.Tensor, n_steps: int, dt: float) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: trajectory [*, n_steps+1, d_z] via Euler-Maruyama (Eq. 6), gradients flow via reparametrisation trick

public_methods.name: spectral_stability_check
public_methods.signature: spectral_stability_check(self, z_samples: torch.Tensor, t_samples: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: diagnostic only -- Lipschitz constant estimate of drift/diffusion networks (paper's Assumption 1 is not numerically enforced, only informally assumed)

module_hierarchy.description: Feynman-Kac PDE residual and market-price-of-risk penalty (Sections 4.2.1-4.2.2, Appendix A.4). NOTE: paper's own Appendix A.4.1 flags this regularizer as theoretically incomplete -- implemented exactly as operationally described in Algorithm 1 / Eq. 7, with the caveat documented in code comments and README.

public_methods.name: forward
public_methods.signature: forward(self, z: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: V_psi(z,t) scalar per collocation point; architecture (depth/width) not specified in paper -- ASSUMED 2-layer MLP matching drift/diffusion net scale

public_methods.name: feynman_kac_residual
public_methods.signature: feynman_kac_residual(self, V: AuxiliaryPricingNet, sde: 'NeuralSDE', z: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: R_FK via torch.autograd.grad (dV/dt, grad_z V, tr(sigma sigma^T grad_z^2 V)), r=0 (Eq. 7)

public_methods.name: pde_loss
public_methods.signature: pde_loss(self, residuals: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: L_PDE = mean(residuals^2)

public_methods.name: market_price_of_risk
public_methods.signature: market_price_of_risk(self, mu: torch.Tensor, sigma: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: lambda(t) = sigma^{-1} @ mu (Eq. 8), via torch.linalg.solve for numerical stability instead of explicit inverse

public_methods.name: mpr_loss
public_methods.signature: mpr_loss(self, lam: torch.Tensor, kappa: float = 2.0) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: L_MPR = mean(relu(||lambda||^2 - kappa^2)) (Eq. 9); kappa=2.0 default per paper's Appendix A.4.2 'reasonable choice' example -- ASSUMED, SIR confidence 0.4, exposed as config param

module_hierarchy.description: Module 3: differentiable symbolic regression layer (Section 4.3, Appendix A.6). Basis-library composition is ASSUMED (paper never enumerates it) -- SIR confidence 0.3.

public_methods.name: compute_features
public_methods.signature: compute_features(self, x_window: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: [*, K] basis features: moving averages (lags 2,5,10,20), pairwise ratios, first differences, rolling variance, per feature channel. K depends on n_channels * n_basis_types -- ASSUMED enumeration, documented in README as not paper-specified

public_methods.name: forward
public_methods.signature: forward(self, basis_features: torch.Tensor, hard: bool = False) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: y_hat_symb = sum_k w_k * f_k(x) (Eq. 12), optional Gumbel-Softmax selection over candidate lag parameterizations (tau annealed toward 0)

public_methods.name: l1_penalty
public_methods.signature: l1_penalty(self, lambda_symb: float) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: L_symb = lambda_symb * ||w||_1 (Eq. 13)

public_methods.name: export_formula
public_methods.signature: export_formula(self, feature_names: list[str], top_k: int = 10) -> str
public_methods.returns: human-readable string of the top-|w_k| weighted terms -- addresses the paper's own gap (no example formula ever shown in the paper)

module_hierarchy.description: Module 4: adaptive conformal prediction + differentiable Kelly-criterion portfolio QP (Section 4.4, Appendix A.7).

public_methods.name: calibrate
public_methods.signature: calibrate(self, residuals: np.ndarray) -> None
public_methods.returns: stores rolling window of residuals

public_methods.name: predict_interval
public_methods.signature: predict_interval(self, y_hat: float, alpha: float = 0.1, window: int = 500) -> tuple[float,float]
public_methods.returns: [y_hat - q, y_hat + q], q = (1-alpha)-quantile of rolling window (Eq. 17); window size ASSUMED (not specified in paper), exposed as config param

public_methods.name: forward
public_methods.signature: forward(self, y_hat: torch.Tensor, q: torch.Tensor, gamma: float = 1.0) -> torch.Tensor
public_methods.returns: w* solving max_w w^T y_hat - (gamma/2) w^T diag(q^2) w s.t. 1^Tw=1,w>=0 via cvxpylayers CvxpyLayer (Eq. 20); OPTIONAL module -- paper does not confirm this was used to produce any reported Table 2/3 metric

module_hierarchy.description: Top-level ARTEMIS model assembling all 4 modules + prediction head + total loss (Section 4.6, Algorithm 1).

tensor_flows.name: LaplaceNeuralOperator.forward
steps: x: [N, d_x], t: [N] <- irregularly sampled raw observations
compute kernel kappa(eval_times[j] - t[i]) for all i,j -> [M, N, d_z, d_x]
left-Riemann-sum: z[j] = sum_i kappa(eval_times[j]-t[i]) @ x[i] * delta_t[i] + b(eval_times[j])
b(eval_times[j]) computed via Fourier time-embedding MLP
return z: [M, d_z] regular-grid latent function, ready for SDE solver

tensor_flows.name: NeuralSDE.simulate
steps: z0: [d_z] <- initial latent state from LNO encoder at t=0
for j in 0..n_steps-1:
mu_j = DriftNet(z_j, t_j) -> [d_z]
sigma_j = DiffusionNet(z_j, t_j) -> [d_z, d_w] (Cholesky L_phi @ D_phi)
eps_j ~ N(0, I_{d_w})
z_{j+1} = z_j + mu_j * dt + sigma_j @ eps_j * sqrt(dt)  (reparametrised, differentiable)
return z_traj: [n_steps+1, d_z] full simulated trajectory

tensor_flows.name: PhysicsLosses.feynman_kac_residual
steps: z, t: [N_coll, d_z], [N_coll] <- sampled from latent trajectories (random times along each simulated path)
V = AuxiliaryPricingNet(z, t) -> [N_coll], requires_grad enabled on z,t
dV_dt = autograd.grad(V.sum(), t, create_graph=True) -> [N_coll]
grad_z_V = autograd.grad(V.sum(), z, create_graph=True) -> [N_coll, d_z]
hessian_z_V = batched autograd.grad of grad_z_V w.r.t. z -> [N_coll, d_z, d_z]
mu = DriftNet(z,t), sigma = DiffusionNet(z,t)
R_FK = dV_dt + (mu * grad_z_V).sum(-1) + 0.5 * trace(sigma @ sigma.T @ hessian_z_V)
return R_FK: [N_coll]; L_PDE = mean(R_FK**2)

tensor_flows.name: SymbolicBottleneck.forward
steps: basis_features: [B, K] <- BasisLibrary.compute_features applied to raw input window
if hard: select top-weighted basis functions via Gumbel-Softmax(logits, tau) -> [K] soft/hard selection mask
y_hat_symb = (basis_features * w[None,:]).sum(-1) -> [B]
return y_hat_symb: [B]; L_symb = lambda_symb * ||w||_1

artemis.d_z: 64  # ASSUMED from Figure 3 caption ('64-dimensional latent space') -- SIR confidence 0.35, not confirmed as the value used in Table 2/3 training
artemis.d_w: 16  # ASSUMED -- no numeric value anywhere in paper
artemis.auxiliary_pricing_hidden_dim: 128  # ASSUMED, architecture entirely unspecified in paper
artemis.n_sde_steps: 100  # ASSUMED from Figure 2 caption ('100-timestep input window') -- SIR confidence 0.3, not confirmed as training-time M

loss_weights.lambda_pde: 1.0  # ASSUMED -- paper only says 'same order of magnitude as forecast loss during early training', no numeric value given

artemis.mpr_kappa: 2.0  # per paper's Appendix A.4.2 'reasonable choice' example for daily data -- ASSUMED as global default, SIR confidence 0.4

symbolic.basis_lags: [2, 5, 10, 20]  # ASSUMED enumeration -- paper never lists the actual basis library

conformal.alpha: 0.1  # ASSUMED (90% coverage) -- not specified in paper

training.batch_size: 64  # ASSUMED -- not specified in paper for any model

runtime.reason: core model implementation, autograd for PDE residuals, Euler-Maruyama SDE simulation via reparametrisation
reference/validation implementation of Euler-Maruyama SDE solving with adjoint gradients, useful to cross-check the custom simulate() loop
loading pretrained Chronos-2 checkpoint via Hugging Face (explicitly named in paper Section 5.5)
differentiable convex optimization layer for the Kelly-criterion portfolio QP (Appendix A.7.3, citing Agrawal et al. 2019)
Jane Street/Optiver/Time-IMM data loading and windowing
Wilcoxon signed-rank test, Spearman RankIC, GARCH/change-point fitting utilities for DSLOB generation
GARCH(1,1) fitting for DSLOB synthetic volatility dynamics (Section 3.4)
CUSUM/Bayesian change-point detection to identify the DSLOB crash window (Section 3.4)
reproducing Figures 2-6 (drift/diffusion profiles, PCA vector fields, benchmark scatter/line plots, ablation loss curves)
PCA for Figure 3's latent-space projection, standardisation utilities

entrypoints.purpose: Run dataset-specific preprocessing (Section 3) for one of jane_street/optiver/time_imm/dslob, producing cached windowed tensors.

entrypoints.purpose: Train ARTEMIS or any one baseline (LSTM/Transformer/NS-Transformer/Informer/Chronos-2) on a chosen dataset.
Run the 7-variant DSLOB ablation study (Table 3: A0_Full through A6_MLP).

cli_args.help: per Figure 6, ablation runs use 10 epochs

entrypoints.purpose: Compute RMSE/RankIC/DirAcc/Weighted-R2 for a trained checkpoint on the test split, and Wilcoxon significance vs. a baseline across seeds.

cli_args.help: comma-separated checkpoint paths across seeds, for Wilcoxon test

entrypoints.purpose: Reproduce Figures 2-6 (drift/diffusion profiles, PCA vector fields, DSLOB regime-degradation plots, prediction scatter, ablation loss curves).

risk_assessment.description: Core architecture hyperparameters -- latent dimension d_z, Wiener dimension d_w, SDE step count M, all four loss weights lambda_1..4, and the symbolic basis-library composition -- are never given numeric values anywhere in the paper. The only concrete hints are two figure captions (Fig. 3: '64-dimensional latent space'; Fig. 2: '100-timestep input window'), neither confirmed as the actual training-time configuration used to produce Table 2/3. Any reproduction attempt is therefore fitting a materially under-specified model.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Every one of these values is exposed as an explicit, clearly-commented '# ASSUMED' config parameter (never hardcoded) so a user with access to the original authors' hyperparameters can override them directly. README documents each assumption and its confidence level prominently.

risk_assessment.description: DSLOB, the dataset used for the paper's central ablation study (Table 3) and headline directional-accuracy result (64.96%), is a synthetic dataset generated by this paper and is explicitly marked non-publicly-available in the SIR. It cannot be obtained -- only regenerated from a description that itself depends on an unspecified 'seed high-frequency limit order book dataset' the paper does not name or provide.
risk_assessment.source: sir.evaluation_protocol.datasets[3].publicly_available=false; Section 3.4 says 'The foundation of DSLOB is a real high-frequency limit order book dataset' without naming which one
risk_assessment.mitigation: Ship a from-scratch synthetic-seed generator (data/make_synthetic_seed_lob.py) that produces a plausible seed LOB series with a hand-specified crash window, so the full DSLOB generation pipeline (Vasicek mid-price, GARCH(1,1) volatility, VAR(1)-correlated noise, time-warping) is exercisable end-to-end for code validation. Explicitly label all such runs as NOT reproducing the paper's actual DSLOB dataset, since the true seed data is unidentified.

risk_assessment.description: The Feynman-Kac PDE regularizer's own theoretical derivation (Appendix A.4.1) is explicitly incomplete by the authors' own admission -- penalizing a single auxiliary V_psi's residual under the physical-measure drift does not actually enforce the no-arbitrage condition the paper claims it enforces (that would require knowledge of the risk-neutral drift mu^Q, which is not estimated anywhere in the described procedure).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement L_PDE exactly as operationally specified in Algorithm 1 (this is what the ablation study in Table 3 actually measures the effect of), but include a prominent code comment and README section quoting the paper's own caveat, so users understand this loss term is empirically validated (ablation shows it matters enormously for directional accuracy) but not proven to enforce true no-arbitrage in the theoretical sense the name suggests.

risk_assessment.description: Two paper-internal numeric inconsistencies affect reproduction: (a) XGBoost is named as a 6th baseline in the abstract but never appears in Table 2 or any baseline description, so it is unclear whether to implement a 6th baseline; (b) DSLOB's feature count is stated as both 59 (Section 5.1 prose) and 85 (Table 1, Section 3.4 detailed description).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement only the 5 baselines that are actually described and reported (LSTM, Transformer, NS-Transformer, Informer, Chronos-2); document the XGBoost discrepancy in README rather than silently implementing a 6th baseline that cannot be validated against any reported number. Use Table 1's 85-feature DSLOB specification as authoritative (corroborated independently by Section 3.4's detailed breakdown) and flag the '59' in Section 5.1 as a likely paper typo.

risk_assessment.description: The Optiver dataset requires fusing two asynchronous data streams (order-book snapshots + trade records) into a regular 1Hz, 600-step sequence via forward-filling -- a non-trivial preprocessing pipeline where subtle bugs (e.g. off-by-one in the forward-fill window boundary, incorrect trade aggregation) could silently produce a plausible-looking but numerically wrong feature set, which would be very hard to detect without the ground-truth Optiver competition data to validate against.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement OptiverPreprocessor.build_dataset with extensive shape/range assertions at each stage (e.g. assert output covers all 600 seconds with no NaNs after forward-fill) and a dedicated data-validation test in tests/ that checks feature statistics against the ranges described in Section 3.2 (e.g. log-transformed realized volatility should look approximately Gaussian).

risk_assessment.description: The Kelly-criterion differentiable portfolio optimization layer (Appendix A.7.3) is described but the paper never confirms whether it was actually exercised to produce any of the reported Table 2/3 metrics -- it may be a described-but-unused optional capability.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement KellyPortfolioLayer as an explicitly optional, disabled-by-default module (use_conformal_allocation controls only interval prediction; the Kelly QP itself is a separate opt-in flag), and do not include its output in any of the reproduced Table 2/3 metrics, since those metrics (RMSE, RankIC, DirAcc, Weighted R2) are all point-prediction metrics that do not depend on portfolio weights.

risk_assessment.description: Symbolic bottleneck basis-function library composition is never enumerated in the paper, and no example distilled formula is shown anywhere despite this being marketed as one of the paper's four headline contributions ('interpretable trading rules').
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement a reasonable, clearly-documented default basis library (moving averages at lags [2,5,10,20], pairwise ratios, first differences, rolling variance, per input channel) and add an export_formula() method that prints the actual distilled top-weighted terms after training, directly addressing the paper's own documentation gap rather than silently reproducing it.
